lessons.title: Debian-Family: dpkg, APT, and the .deb
lessons.content:
On Debian and Ubuntu there are **two layers**. `dpkg` is the low-level tool that unpacks and registers a single `.deb` file; it knows nothing about where packages come from and will not fetch dependencies. **APT** (`apt`, `apt-get`, `apt-cache`) is the high-level layer that talks to repositories, resolves dependencies, and then calls `dpkg` to do the actual install.

A `.deb` is just an `ar` archive containing `control.tar` (metadata: name, version, `Depends:`, maintainer scripts) and `data.tar` (the files to install). You can crack one open without installing it:

```bash
ar t mytool.deb              # list members
dpkg-deb -I mytool.deb       # show control info
dpkg-deb -c mytool.deb       # list files it would install
```

Day-to-day commands:

```bash
sudo apt update              # refresh the package index
sudo apt install nginx       # resolve deps + install
apt-cache policy nginx       # candidate version + repo source
sudo dpkg -i mytool.deb      # install a local .deb (no deps!)
sudo apt install -f          # then fix missing deps
```

Repositories are configured in `/etc/apt/sources.list` and `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/*.list` (or the newer deb822 `.sources` format). Each line names a mirror URL, a suite (e.g. `bookworm`), and components (`main`, `contrib`). `apt update` downloads the **index** from each; `apt install` then knows what is available. The mental model that matters most: `apt` is online and dependency-aware, `dpkg` is local and dumb. Reach for `dpkg` only when you already have a `.deb` in hand or need to inspect the package database directly.

items.front: What is the division of labor between `dpkg` and `apt`?
items.back: `dpkg` is the low-level engine: it unpacks/configures a single local `.deb` and records it in the package database, but does not fetch dependencies. `apt` is the high-level front end: it talks to repositories, resolves dependencies, downloads packages, then invokes `dpkg` to install them.
items.hint: One is online and dependency-aware; one is local and dumb.

items.front: A `.deb` file is fundamentally what kind of archive, and what are its two main payload members?
items.back: It is an `ar` archive. The two payloads are `control.tar.*` (metadata: package name, version, `Depends:`, maintainer scripts) and `data.tar.*` (the actual files to be installed onto the filesystem). A `debian-binary` member holds the format version.
items.hint: Same `ar` format used for static libraries; metadata vs. files.

items.front: After `sudo dpkg -i tool.deb` fails complaining about missing dependencies, what single command repairs it?
items.back: `sudo apt install -f` (or `apt --fix-broken install`). `dpkg` installed the package but cannot fetch deps; `apt -f` reads the broken state, downloads the missing dependencies from the repos, and completes the configuration.
items.hint: Let the online layer clean up after the dumb one.

items.front: Where are APT repositories configured, and what three things does a sources entry specify?
items.back: In `/etc/apt/sources.list` and `/etc/apt/sources.list.d/*.list` (or deb822 `.sources` files). Each entry specifies the mirror URL, the suite/distribution (e.g. `bookworm`), and the components (e.g. `main contrib non-free`).

items.prompt: You want to see every file a local `tool.deb` would install WITHOUT installing it. Which command?
items.explanation: `dpkg-deb -c` lists the contents of a `.deb` file on disk. `dpkg -L` lists files of an already-INSTALLED package (and takes a package name, not a file). `apt-cache show` reads index metadata, not a local file's file list.

items.prompt: What does `sudo apt update` actually do?
choices: Upgrades all installed packages to their newest versions
Refreshes the local copy of the repositories' package indexes
items.explanation: `apt update` only re-downloads and verifies the package INDEX from each configured repo, so APT knows what versions exist. Upgrading installed packages is `apt upgrade` / `apt full-upgrade` — a separate step.

items.prompt: Which tool will fetch and install a package's dependencies automatically?
items.explanation: Only `apt install` resolves and downloads dependencies from repositories. `dpkg -i` installs one local file without fetching deps; `dpkg-deb -x` and `ar x` merely extract the archive.

items.prompt: `apt-cache policy nginx` is most useful for answering which question?
choices: What candidate version is available and from which repo?
items.explanation: `apt-cache policy` shows the installed version, the candidate (what would be installed), and the priority-ranked list of repositories/mirrors offering it. It is the go-to tool when you are unsure where a version is coming from.

lessons.title: RPM/dnf, pacman, Signing, and Owning Files
lessons.content:
The same two-layer idea reappears across families. **Red Hat/Fedora**: `rpm` is the low-level tool, `dnf` (formerly `yum`) is the dependency-resolving front end; repos live in `/etc/yum.repos.d/*.repo`. **Arch**: `pacman` is a single tool that does both, with repos in `/etc/pacman.conf` and the AUR for community builds. Translating between families is mostly vocabulary:

```bash
# Debian            # RHEL/Fedora          # Arch
apt install pkg     dnf install pkg        pacman -S pkg
apt remove pkg      dnf remove pkg         pacman -R pkg
apt update          dnf check-update       pacman -Sy
dpkg -L pkg         rpm -ql pkg            pacman -Ql pkg
dpkg -S /path/file  rpm -qf /path/file     pacman -Qo /path/file
```

Two cross-cutting skills matter most in practice. **Which package owns a file?** When a stray binary or config appears, ask the database: `dpkg -S /usr/bin/curl`, `rpm -qf /usr/bin/curl`, or `pacman -Qo`. The inverse — list everything a package installed — is `dpkg -L`, `rpm -ql`, `pacman -Ql`.

**Package signing and trust.** This is the security backbone. Every repo publishes a signed index; your machine holds the repo's public **GPG key**. APT verifies the signature on the `InRelease`/`Release` file, which in turn lists the cryptographic hashes of every package — so a tampered or man-in-the-middle package fails verification. RPM signs each package directly (`rpm --checksig`). The crucial mental model: **you trust a key, not a server.** A compromised mirror cannot forge a validly signed package. Modern Debian keeps third-party keys in `/etc/apt/keyrings/` and references them per-source with `signed-by=`, rather than the deprecated global `apt-key`, so one vendor's key cannot vouch for another's packages.

items.front: Across Debian, RHEL, and Arch, what command answers "which installed package owns this file?"
items.back: Debian: `dpkg -S /path/to/file`. RHEL/Fedora: `rpm -qf /path/to/file`. Arch: `pacman -Qo /path/to/file`. All query the local package database to map a file back to its owning package.

items.front: What command lists ALL files installed by an already-installed package on each family?
items.back: Debian: `dpkg -L pkg`. RHEL/Fedora: `rpm -ql pkg`. Arch: `pacman -Ql pkg`. This is the inverse of the "who owns this file?" query.

items.front: In the package-signing security model, what exactly do you trust, and why does that defeat a malicious mirror?
items.back: You trust a maintainer's/repo's GPG public key, not the server hosting the files. The signed index commits to the hash of every package, so any tampered package (even from a compromised mirror or MITM) fails signature/hash verification. A mirror cannot produce a validly signed index without the private key.
items.hint: Trust the key, not the host.

items.front: Why is modern APT's `signed-by=` keyring approach safer than the old global `apt-key`?
items.back: With global `apt-key`, any key in the global trust set could vouch for ANY repository. With per-source `signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/vendor.gpg`, a given key only authorizes its own repo, so one third party's key cannot silently authenticate packages from another source. It scopes trust.
items.hint: Scoping trust to one repo instead of all.

items.prompt: A mysterious binary `/usr/bin/widget` appears on a Fedora box. Which command tells you which package put it there?
items.explanation: `rpm -qf FILE` (query-file) maps a file path to its owning package. `rpm -ql` is the inverse (list a package's files) and expects a package name. `rpm -V` verifies a package against its recorded metadata.

items.prompt: Why can a compromised package mirror NOT slip a backdoored package onto a correctly configured Debian system?
choices: Because APT downloads everything over HTTPS only
Because the signed index commits to each package's hash, and the mirror lacks the private signing key
Because dpkg refuses to install anything from a mirror
items.explanation: Trust rests on the GPG signature of the repo index, which lists each package's hash. Without the private key the mirror cannot forge a valid signature, and an altered package won't match the signed hash. HTTPS alone does not provide this (mirrors are often plain HTTP); trust is in the key.

items.prompt: Which mapping correctly translates `apt install pkg` to Arch?
items.explanation: `pacman -S pkg` syncs/installs a package (with dependencies) from the configured repos — the analogue of `apt install`. `-R` removes, `-Qo` queries the owner of a file, and `-Sy` alone just refreshes the database.

items.prompt: On RHEL/Fedora, what is the relationship between `rpm` and `dnf`?
choices: They are unrelated; dnf is for containers only
`rpm` resolves dependencies and `dnf` installs single files
`dnf` resolves dependencies from repos and drives `rpm` for the low-level install
`dnf` replaced `rpm` entirely and rpm no longer exists
items.explanation: Just like dpkg/apt on Debian: `rpm` is the low-level package tool, while `dnf` (successor to yum) is the high-level resolver that pulls from repos and ultimately invokes the rpm machinery. Both still exist and have distinct roles.

lessons.title: Source Builds, Flatpak/Snap/Nix, and Security Updates
lessons.content:
Sometimes no package exists, so you build from source. The classic Autotools dance:

```bash
./configure --prefix=/usr/local   # probe system, generate Makefile
make -j$(nproc)                    # compile
sudo make install                  # copy artifacts into --prefix
```

The trap: `make install` scatters files with no database tracking them, so there is no clean uninstall and no dependency bookkeeping. That pain is *why packaging exists*. Prefer `--prefix=/usr/local` (or tools like `checkinstall`/`stow`) to keep hand-built software out of `/usr` where the package manager rules.

**The system-vs-language tension.** Language managers — `pip`/`venv`, `npm`, `cargo` — install into their own ecosystems and ignore the OS database. Mixing `sudo pip install` with apt-managed Python is a classic way to break a system, because both fight over the same `/usr/lib/python*`. The discipline: keep language deps in **virtual environments** / project-local dirs (`venv`, `node_modules`, `~/.cargo`), and let the system manager own the system. This unresolved tension is a major motivation for **containers** (Module 14).

**Modern universal formats** sidestep the conflict by bundling dependencies and sandboxing:
- **Flatpak** — desktop apps, runtime + sandbox (Flathub), portals for access.
- **Snap** — Canonical's confined packages with auto-update; uses squashfs.
- **AppImage** — one self-contained executable file, no install step.
- **Nix** — purely functional, content-addressed store (`/nix/store`) giving reproducible, atomically rollback-able environments.

**Staying patched.** Apply security updates promptly: `apt upgrade` / `dnf upgrade`, and on Debian/Ubuntu enable `unattended-upgrades` for automatic security patches. Use **version pinning/holds** (`apt-mark hold pkg`, dnf `versionlock`, APT pin priorities) to freeze a known-good version, but audit held packages — a forgotten hold is a lingering vulnerability.

items.front: What is the classic three-step Autotools build-from-source sequence, and what does each step do?
items.back: `./configure` probes the system and generates a Makefile (set install location with `--prefix`); `make` compiles the sources; `sudo make install` copies the built artifacts into the prefix. The downside: nothing records the installed files, so there is no clean uninstall or dependency tracking.

items.front: Why is `sudo pip install <pkg>` system-wide considered risky on a modern distro?
items.back: The system Python is managed by apt/dnf. A system-wide pip install writes into the same `/usr/lib/python*` paths, can overwrite or shadow distro-managed files, and creates a state the OS package manager doesn't know about — leading to breakage on upgrades. Use a `venv` / project-local install instead.
items.hint: Two managers fighting over the same files.

items.front: Contrast how Flatpak/Snap/AppImage handle dependencies versus a traditional .deb/.rpm.
items.back: Traditional packages share libraries with the rest of the system and rely on the package manager to resolve dependencies. Flatpak/Snap/AppImage instead BUNDLE their dependencies (and typically run sandboxed/confined), trading larger size and duplicated libs for isolation, portability across distros, and freedom from the host's dependency state.

items.front: What makes Nix's approach to package management reproducible and rollback-friendly?
items.back: Nix installs every build into a content-addressed, immutable store (`/nix/store/<hash>-name`). Because each package and its exact dependency closure has a unique path, multiple versions coexist without conflict, builds are reproducible, and switching/rolling back an environment is just repointing symlinks atomically.
items.hint: Immutable hashed store + atomic profile switches.

items.front: How do you freeze a Debian package at its current version, and what is the maintenance hazard?
items.back: `sudo apt-mark hold <pkg>` (RHEL: dnf `versionlock`; APT also supports pin priorities in `/etc/apt/preferences.d/`). The hazard: a held package is excluded from upgrades including SECURITY updates, so a forgotten hold becomes a silent, lingering vulnerability. Audit holds periodically (`apt-mark showhold`).
items.hint: hold/versionlock — but it also blocks security patches.

items.prompt: What is the main reason `make install` is inferior to using a package for the same software?
choices: It compiles slower than the package build
It leaves untracked files with no clean uninstall or dependency management
items.explanation: `make install` just copies files into the prefix; no database records them, so removal is manual and error-prone and dependencies aren't tracked. Solving exactly this bookkeeping problem is why package managers exist.

items.prompt: You need a Python data tool for one project without disturbing the apt-managed system Python. Best practice?
choices: sudo pip install into the system site-packages
Create a venv and pip install into it
apt remove python3 then reinstall via pip
items.explanation: A virtual environment (`python -m venv .venv`) isolates the project's packages from the system Python entirely, avoiding the system-vs-language conflict. The other options corrupt or destabilize the OS-managed interpreter.

items.prompt: Which universal package format is a single self-contained executable file that requires no installation step?
items.explanation: An AppImage bundles the app and its libraries into one executable file you can `chmod +x` and run anywhere. Flatpak and Snap install through their own daemons/runtimes; Nix uses a store under /nix.

items.prompt: On Debian/Ubuntu, which mechanism automatically applies security updates without manual intervention?
items.explanation: `unattended-upgrades` is the package/service that periodically installs security (and optionally other) updates automatically. `apt-mark hold` does the opposite (freezes a package); the others inspect or reconfigure state.
